title: Bolognese

overview.description: A rich, slow-cooked Italian meat sauce built on a soffritto, good quality beef and plenty of time. Perfect served with pasta or as the foundation for lasagne and many other family favourites.

method: Heat the olive oil in a large heavy-based saucepan over a medium heat.
Cook the onion, carrot and celery for 15–20 minutes until very soft and sweet but not browned.
Add the garlic and cook for 1 minute.
Add the beef mince and cook until well browned, breaking it up as it cooks.
Stir in the tomato purée and cook for 2 minutes.
Add the chopped tomatoes, beef stock, milk and bay leaves.
Season lightly with salt, pepper and oregano.
Cook uncovered for 2–3 hours, stirring occasionally, until rich, thick and deeply flavoured.
Remove the bay leaves before serving.

learning.did-you-know: Traditional Bolognese (Ragù alla Bolognese) is slowly simmered and often includes milk, which softens the acidity of the tomatoes and enriches the finished sauce.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to build flavour from a soffritto, why patience is essential for a great ragù and how slow cooking transforms simple ingredients.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: This is one of the foundation recipes of the entire cookbook. A single batch becomes multiple future meals and freezes beautifully.
chapman-tips: Don't rush the soffritto. It provides much of the sweetness in the finished sauce.
If you have homemade beef stock, use it.
If you've recently made the Jamie Oliver Beef Short Ribs, replace some or all of the beef stock with the reserved cooking liquor for an even richer sauce.
The sauce is even better the following day.
notes.batch-cooking: Divide into meal-sized portions and freeze for up to three months.
notes.leftovers: Perfect for lasagne, pasta bakes, stuffed vegetables or simply reheated with fresh pasta.
